lens.included_activities: US LMM firms that repeatedly convert plastics resin or film into bags and pouches for external food, healthcare, personal-care, retail, institutional, industrial, and other customers, including forming, coating, plastics-to-plastics laminating, printing, and pouch converting performed within the establishment.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive packaging operations, printing-only firms using purchased packaging, paper or foil combination bags classified outside 326111, film-only producers, pure distributors, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B sale of specified physical packaging, typically driven by customer production volumes, purchase orders, qualification requirements, artwork and format changes, and recurring supply agreements; resin cost changes may be passed through contractually or at renewal.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment by occupation for all NAICS 326100 plastics-product manufacturing, not 326111 or a wage-weighted LMM task mix, while NIST describes cross-manufacturing AI adoption and use cases. I use the high production share to cap exposure and assign higher exposure to inspection, scheduling, maintenance analytics, quoting, artwork and order processing, procurement, customer service, and administration. The proxy may understate bag converters' repetitive visual-inspection opportunity but overstate it if modern lines already automate inspection, counting, and material handling.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 58.94% of plastics-product employment, with 25.97% in metal and plastic worker occupations and 5.17% as inspectors and testers. Core extrusion, web handling, sealing, changeover, maintenance, material movement, and physical quality checks constrain pure software substitution, but vision inspection, predictive maintenance, scheduling, demand forecasting, order and artwork workflow, and office tasks create meaningful exposure. NIST identifies these as active manufacturing AI use cases.
a.change_evidence: A 326111 wage-weighted SOC file plus task-level time studies at ten bag and pouch plants, identifying current automation, manual inspection and changeover hours, scheduling and quoting labor, maintenance records, and administrative workflows, would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupation proxy includes all plastics products and may overrepresent molding and assembly relative to film converting.
BLS employment shares are not wage-weighted and do not indicate tasks already automated.
NIST adoption evidence spans US manufacturing rather than flexible packaging.

rho.bridge: NIST measures current and expected AI use across US manufacturers rather than completed five-year implementation of exposed tasks in 326111. I translate its reported adoption, use cases, and barriers into a moderate implementation share, reduced for LMM capital limits, legacy converting lines, integration, food-contact validation, cybersecurity, customer approval, and workforce skills. This could overstate implementation if the cited manufacturers are larger and more digitally mature than LMM converters.
rho.rationale: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting to increase use within two years, with inspection, predictive maintenance, inventory, scheduling, document management, and material handling among named use cases. Bag converting is repetitive and sensor-friendly, so machine vision and planning tools can be deployed within five years, but model reliability, web-speed integration, sparse defect data, legacy controls, high initial cost, and quality accountability prevent full conversion.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor and operator data from machine-vision, web-inspection, maintenance, and production-scheduling deployments at LMM flexible-packaging plants, including uptime, false-reject rates, labor hours, avoided hires, capex, and payback, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: The reported AI adoption figures include chatbots and do not measure labor implementation.
The range assumes plants can obtain reliable line and quality data without wholesale equipment replacement.
Food, medical, and other regulated packaging require more validation than commodity retail bags.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is a coherent converting activity, and bags and pouches are consumable inputs that customers reorder as they produce, ship, sell, or dispose of goods. Eligibility is reduced for captive plants, commodity spot producers without durable repeat relationships, pure distribution, single-program or owner-dependent operations, and firms whose normalized EBITDA or compliance condition does not support a transferable control business.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the 117 injected LMM candidates, classifying external-customer revenue, repeat-order share, contract duration, end-market mix, customer concentration, captive status, and normalized EBITDA, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source directly measures eligibility in the LMM 326111 population.
Physical product supply is treated as repeat outsourced provision under the fixed lens.
The injected firm count uses a margin bridge from paper and forest products and may misclassify the LMM band.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US employer businesses of all industries and sizes, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM plastics bag and pouch manufacturers. I center near its 22% employer-owner result, allowing upward error from packaging consolidation and older manufacturing ownership and downward error from failed processes, internal transfers, environmental diligence, and customer concentration.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people at least 55 years old and 22% of employer owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. The fragmented 117-firm estimated population and packaging consolidation support material availability, while capex requirements, resin volatility, regulation, and customer approvals can impair transaction completion.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, packaging-industry brokers, and SBA change-of-ownership lending, tagged to 326111 EBITDA, owner age, control status, and failed-sale reasons, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes firms much smaller than the LMM band.
Survey intentions include gifts and other transfers that may not qualify.
Large-company consolidation is evidence of buyer interest but not an LMM probability measure.

q.rationale: Savings in scrap, downtime, yield, artwork errors, and planning can be partly retained where converters provide qualified high-barrier structures, short runs, service, printing, or complex pouches. Retention is limited by competitive bidding, transparent conversion economics, large consumer-goods buyers, frequent repricing, and contractual resin pass-through. Berry states that price is a significant competitive dimension and raw-material price changes are generally passed through over time or at renewal, showing why visible cost reductions may be shared.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and invoice histories showing resin and energy escalators, open-book terms, bid cadence, renewal pricing, rebates, and actual price response after scrap or labor reductions would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Berry is much larger and more diversified than the LMM lens.
Retention is lower for commodity bags and higher for qualified food, medical, printed, or high-barrier pouches.
Demand loss and implementation failure are excluded from q.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader NAICS 326100 plastics-product industry, not 326111 constant-quality bag and pouch demand, while Berry represents a large global flexible and rigid packaging supplier and California regulation covers one state and a broader packaging population. I translate the 1.1% annual broader-industry output projection into a modest five-year growth anchor, then widen and lower the base for source reduction, reuse, paper substitution, lightweighting, and bag restrictions. The proxy may understate resilient food and healthcare pouches or overstate single-use retail and institutional bags.
d5.rationale: BLS projects plastics-product manufacturing output rising from 190.7 to 212.4 billion chained 2017 dollars over 2024-2034, a 1.1% annual rate. Berry identifies stable food, beverage, healthcare, and personal-care end markets and packaging roles in protection and shelf life. Against that, California's rules target 25% less plastic by 2032, nearly half through elimination or reuse/refill, and similar policies, lightweighting, and material substitution can reduce units or resin intensity. The mixed five-year base is roughly flat real quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Census product-level bag and pouch shipment quantities, BLS producer-price indexes, state-by-state single-use and EPR implementation forecasts, and customer packaging-conversion roadmaps by food, healthcare, retail, and industrial end market would materially move the range.
caveats: BLS output covers all plastics products, not bags and pouches.
Company disclosures mix regions and flexible products beyond 326111.
Regulation may reduce resin weight while preserving or increasing the number and value of more engineered pouches.

o.rationale: Year-five demand that remains for plastic bags and pouches requires a physical converter to extrude or source film, print, laminate, form, seal, test, and deliver packaging. AI can automate design, inspection, scheduling, and order workflows, while large customers can vertically integrate and reuse systems can displace some converting, but software alone cannot provide the remaining physical packaging.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with major food, healthcare, consumer-products, retail, and industrial packaging buyers quantifying insourcing, reusable-system adoption, direct-from-film conversion, and supplier consolidation over five years would materially change the range.
caveats: The value is conditional on the quantity remaining in d5; eliminated single-use demand is not counted twice.
Reusable or refill systems may shift demand to operators outside 326111 rather than remove accountable supply entirely.

narrative.executive_view: Plastic bag and pouch converting is a coherent, repeat-supply industry with substantial physical production labor. AI can improve inspection, maintenance, scheduling, quoting, order and artwork handling, and inventory decisions, but competitive pricing and plastic-reduction rules constrain retained value and demand.
narrative.ai_impact: High-speed webs, repetitive defects, expensive scrap, unplanned downtime, frequent changeovers, and complex order specifications create practical machine-vision and predictive-analytics use cases. The largest labor effects are likely in quality review, planning, maintenance support, customer workflow, and administration rather than eliminating line operators or technicians.
narrative.value_capture: Converters retain more where barrier performance, food or medical qualification, printing, pouch complexity, short runs, and service create switching costs. Commodity bags face strong price competition, and resin escalators or contract renewals make cost economics visible to customers, encouraging sharing of savings.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected population is 117 estimated LMM firms, and most should have repeat external orders. Target-level diligence must still exclude captive, distribution-heavy, spot-commodity, highly concentrated, owner-dependent, and compliance-deficient operations and verify that the paper-industry margin bridge identifies the EBITDA band accurately.
narrative.demand_durability: Food, healthcare, personal-care, industrial, and protective uses sustain packaging demand and benefit from low weight and shelf-life performance. Single-use restrictions, EPR fees, reuse and refill systems, lightweighting, paper substitution, recycled-content constraints, and customer sustainability commitments create a material downside tail.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risks are regulatory source reduction, resin and energy volatility, customer concentration, commoditization, capital intensity, quality recalls, food-contact requirements, and the possibility that large buyers capture most productivity gains. Evidence is mostly four-digit occupations and output plus large-company disclosures rather than LMM 326111 observations.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring physical packaging demand and sensor-rich converting lines make targeted AI-enabled quality, uptime, and planning improvements plausible.
narrative.principal_weakness: Competitive pass-through and accelerating plastic source-reduction mandates can erode both retained savings and demand.

sources.title: 2017 NAICS Definition File: 326111 Plastics Bag and Pouch Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as converting resin or forming, coating, or plastics-to-plastics laminating film or sheet into bags and pouches, with printing allowed in the establishment.

sources.title: May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Plastics Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports production occupations at 58.94% of employment, metal and plastic workers at 25.97%, inspectors and testers at 5.17%, and transportation and material-moving occupations at 10.97%.

sources.title: NIST MEP: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use in two years; identifies inspection, predictive maintenance, scheduling, forecasting, document management, and material handling use cases plus data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system barriers.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years.

sources.title: Berry Global Group, Inc. 2024 Annual Report on Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes flexible films and bags, stable consumer end markets, competition on service, innovation, quality and price, resin-price pass-through, packaging's protection and shelf-life roles, and recyclable or circular-material targets.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects plastics-product manufacturing real output from $190.7 billion to $212.4 billion in chained 2017 dollars over 2024-2034, a 1.1% annual growth rate.

sources.title: CalRecycle: California Approves New Plastic and Packaging Rules
sources.supports: States that producers have until 2032 to achieve 25% less plastic, with nearly half through elimination or reuse and refill, plus recyclable or compostable and recycling targets.
